title: Leaves of Grass
description: Whitman's expansive poetic celebration of democracy, the body, labor, and desire.
descriptionParagraphs: Leaves of Grass is Whitman's long, evolving poetic project of self, nation, body, labor, comradeship, and democratic expansiveness. The poems stretch outward in catalogs, apostrophes, erotic addresses, and prophetic announcements, trying to invent a voice large enough to hold common speech, sensual life, spiritual speculation, and civic aspiration at once.

The work's themes are equality, embodiment, mortality, desire, and the hope that poetry can register the fullness of ordinary existence without hierarchy. Its importance lies in that wager on scale and inclusiveness. Whitman makes lyric poetry sound public, intimate, sexual, and national all at the same time, which is part of why the book was felt as liberating by some readers and scandalous by others.
Whitman's expansive poetic celebration of democracy, the body, labor, and desire.

cover.alt: Leaves of Grass cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: Whitman's expansive poetic celebration of democracy, the body, labor, and desire.
banningParagraphs: Leaves of Grass entered censorship debates as a poetry collection associated with poetry, body, and democracy. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and sexuality.

The earliest event currently captured here is 19th century in United States, where Postal and morals authorities suppressed editions. Whitman's treatment of the body and desire repeatedly drew moral attack. Its censorship history complicates the idea of a uniformly triumphant democratic literary canon.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
